summary: Atwood examines the growing reluctance among programmers to pay for commercial software tools, sparked by a comment stating 'we don't use software that costs money here.' He traces this shift not just to piracy or frugality, but to the rapid improvement of free alternatives like Eclipse, Subversion, MySQL, and Firefox. The post argues that commercial tool vendors can no longer rest on their laurels—they must continuously prove their value proposition against relentlessly improving free competition. Using examples from SmugMug and file comparison tools, Atwood concludes that survival in the face of 'free' requires delivering a genuinely premium experience that justifies the cost.
key_points: Free software alternatives have improved dramatically, with tools like MySQL and PHP evolving from 'toy' status to credible enterprise solutions
The competitive pressure of free products on commercial tools intensifies every year, forcing vendors to continuously innovate
Commercial software can survive by being 'the first, the free, or the good'—and if you're neither first nor free, you must be demonstrably better
Free has its own weaknesses: it tends to be 'cheap, mass-produced, and the same for everyone'
The audience for development tools is uniquely challenging because the people who would buy them are exactly the same people who could build them
money_quotes: We don't use software that costs money here.
These people used to be called pirates. Now they're open source enthusiasts.
The competitive pressure of free products on commercial tools intensifies every year. It's relentless.
Resting on their laurels is a luxury they no longer have.
A free program need not be glamorous or even completely bug-free. It can garner a respectable following simply by not costing anything.
I've seen many times people struggle and struggle on with a clunky freeware app just because they're not willing to pay $20 for a significantly better alternative.
The type of people who would buy development tools are also exactly the same people who could potentially build them.
Free is indeed a competitive advantage. But free is also a weakness: it is cheap, mass-produced, and the same for everyone.
key_insight: Commercial software can only survive against free alternatives by delivering a premium experience that justifies the cost—a challenge that grows harder every year as open source tools relentlessly improve.
